content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
March 16, 1990
HarLan Loeb
Lindquist & vennum
4200 IDS Center
Minneapolis, ttinnesota 55402
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-9 0-117
Dear Mr. Loeb:
You have requested advice concerning the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the PoIitical Reform Act,1
FACTs
You are a 1a$/ clerk with the law firn Lindquist & Vennum. A
portion of your duties includes lobbying on behalf of the National
Association of ReaI Estate Appraisers, a client of Lindquist &
vennun. In a telephone conversation on March 6, 1990, you
indicated that your lobbying activities are a regular portion of
your job responsibilities, 
encompassing approxinately 15 hours per
week. In 1990, you will have contacts with members of the
California Legislature and their staff regarding a proposed bill
which $rould establish reguLatory standards for real estate
appraisers. If the legislation becomes law you anticipate
lobbying activities before the California Departnent of ReaI
Estate.
OI'EATION
Are you required to register as a lobbyist?
CONCLUSION
As discussed in the analysis, you may have regi.stration and
reporting requirements under the Act. In addition, even if your
activities are such that you do not qualify as a lobbyist, the law
firu nay have registration and reporting requirements as a
lobbying firn.
1 
co.r".rr.ent Code Sections 81OOO-91015. AIl statutory
references are to the covernment Code unless othenrise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18OOO, et seq. AIl references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the CaLifornia Code of Regulations.
'128 J Strcct, Strite 8!'t0 . P.O. Box Stl7 o S:rcrirmcntr) C.\ 95ti('\.+-OSr)7 . (91()) ]22-5t,6O


ANAI,YS IA
A lobbyist is defined in the Act as follows:
rrLobbyistrr means any individual who is employed or
contracts for economic consideration, other than
reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, to comnunicate
directly or through his or her agents with any elective state
official, 
agency official 
or legislative official 
for the
purpose of influencing legisJ.ative or administrative action,
if a substantial or reqular portion of the activities for
which he or she receives consideration is for the ouroose of
influencinq lecrislative or adrninistrative action.
(Section 82039 , emphasis added. )
For purposes of deterrnining whether the activities of a
person who engages in direct communication with legislative or
state agency officials 
are t'substantial or reguLar,r' two tests are
set out in Regulation 18239 (copy enclosed):
... (b) The person receives or becomes entitled to
receive at least $Z,ooo in compensation in any calendar month
for influencing legislative or adninistrative action...
(c) The person receives or becomes entitled to receive
anv amount of compensation for enqaqinq in direct
cornmunication, other than adninistrative testimony, vrith
qualifying officials 
for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action on at least 25 seDarate
occasions in anv two consecutive calendar months.
(Enphasis added. )
You indicated during our telephone conversation that you
would not be receiving $2,000 in a calendar month for your
lobbying activities. 
However, it was not clear whether your
activities would meet the ttcontactsrr test established in
Regulation 18239(c). contacts with a1t members of the California
Legislature and their staff (except clerical staff) for the
purpose of attenpting to influence legislative action count toward
the rrcontactsrr test. 
tLegislative actionrr includes:
..,the drafting, introduction, consideration,
ruodif i.cation, enactment or defeat of any biIl, resolution,
anendment, report, nomination or other natter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee,
subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a
nember or employee of the Legislature acting in his official
capacity. 'tLegislative actiont' also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any biII.
(Section 83o37. )
t-
